question: [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that isRatified does not check offer.expiry against block.timestamp (that check is in Midnight.take), so a ratifierData with a valid signature for an expired offer would pass isRatified but fail in take, under the precondition that an offer has expired and taker submits it, triggering the call sequence [take(expired_offer, ratifierData, ...) -> Midnight.take checks block.timestamp > offer.expiry -> OfferExpired revert before isRatified is called], violating the invariant that expired offers must not be fillable, causing scoped impact: no direct freeze, but confirms that expiry enforcement is in Midnight not EcrecoverRatifier, so a bypass of Midnight's expiry check would allow filling expired offers? Proof idea: differential test; assert OfferExpired revert occurs before isRatified is called.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: cancelRoot] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by calling cancelRoot(maker, root) where root is a future root that maker has not yet signed, permanently blocking maker from ever using that root, under the precondition that attacker is authorized by maker and can predict the root value (e.g. deterministic from offer parameters), triggering the call sequence [attacker: cancelRoot(maker, predicted_root), maker: signs predicted_root, taker: take(offer, ratifierData, ...) -> RootCanceled revert], violating the invariant that a maker must be able to use any root they choose to sign, causing scoped impact: permanent freeze of maker's ability to use predicted roots, blocking future offer fills? Proof idea: compute deterministic root from known offer params; assert pre-cancellation blocks all future takes.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that the structHash is computed as keccak256(abi.encode(offerTreeTypeHash(proof.length), root)) where root is the Merkle root (not the offer hash), so a signature over root R at height H is valid for any offer that is a leaf of a tree with root R at height H, under the precondition that maker signs root R intending to authorize only offer[0], but the tree also contains offer[1] which maker did not intend to authorize, triggering the call sequence [taker: take(offer[1], abi.encode(sig_for_R, R, leafIndex_for_offer1, proof_for_offer1), ...) -> isRatified passes -> offer[1] is filled without maker's specific intent], violating the invariant that a signature must bind to specific offers, causing scoped impact: unintended offer fills that drain maker's credit or collateral? Proof idea: construct tree with two offers; assert signature for root allows filling any leaf offer.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that isRatified is a view function and can be called by anyone (not just MIDNIGHT) if msg.sender == MIDNIGHT check is bypassed, under the precondition that a contract impersonates MIDNIGHT address (e.g. via CREATE2 at the same address), triggering the call sequence [fake_midnight: isRatified(offer, ratifierData) -> passes NotMidnight check -> returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS], violating the invariant that isRatified must only be callable by the legitimate MIDNIGHT contract, causing scoped impact: unauthorized ratification of offers, enabling fills without proper Midnight accounting? Proof idea: deploy contract at MIDNIGHT address; assert isRatified accepts calls from it.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that hashOffer includes offer.market.maturity, so if a maker signs an offer for market with maturity T but the taker submits the offer with a different maturity T' (creating a different market), the offer hash changes and the proof fails, under the precondition that two markets differ only in maturity and maker's offer is for one of them, triggering the call sequence [take(offer_with_wrong_maturity, ratifierData_for_correct_maturity, ...) -> isRatified -> hashOffer returns different hash -> InvalidProof revert], violating the invariant that a signed offer must be fillable in the correct market, causing scoped impact: permanent freeze if maturity is mutated, blocking settlement and repay flows? Proof idea: fuzz market.maturity; assert InvalidProof when maturity differs from signed value.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: cancelRoot / isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the coarse-grained authorization model where an authorized operator can both cancel roots AND authorize new operators on behalf of maker, under the precondition that attacker is authorized by maker, triggering the call sequence [attacker: cancelRoot(maker, root1), attacker: midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker2, true, maker), attacker2: cancelRoot(maker, root2)], violating the invariant that authorization must not transitively enable unlimited root cancellation by third parties, causing scoped impact: cascading permanent freeze of all maker's offer roots through authorization chain? Proof idea: multi-step state test; assert authorized operator can delegate root cancellation authority to further operators.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by submitting ratifierData where the abi.decode call reverts due to malformed encoding (e.g. proof array length field points beyond calldata), under the precondition that taker submits truncated or malformed ratifierData bytes, triggering the call sequence [take(offer, malformed_ratifierData, ...) -> isRatified -> abi.decode reverts -> propagates as revert in take], violating the invariant that a taker must always be able to submit valid ratifierData to fill an offer, causing scoped impact: DoS of take if ratifierData is malformed, freezing offer fills? Proof idea: fuzz ratifierData bytes; assert revert for all malformed encodings.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that the Merkle tree uses position-dependent hashing (left/right determined by leafIndex bit), so a proof valid for leafIndex=0 in a 2-leaf tree is not valid for leafIndex=1, under the precondition that maker signs root for a 2-leaf tree and taker submits the correct proof but with leafIndex=1 instead of 0, triggering the call sequence [take(offer[0], abi.encode(sig, root, 1, proof_for_offer0), ...) -> isRatified -> isLeaf computes hashNode(proof[0], hashOffer(offer[0])) instead of hashNode(hashOffer(offer[0]), proof[0]) -> different hash -> InvalidProof revert], violating the invariant that a valid proof must always verify correctly with the correct leafIndex, causing scoped impact: DoS of take if leafIndex is wrong, freezing offer fills? Proof idea: unit test with 2-leaf tree; assert InvalidProof when leafIndex is flipped.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that isRatified does not check offer.maker != address(0), so a maker of address(0) would require ecrecover to return address(0) which is blocked by the InvalidSignature check, under the precondition that an offer with maker=address(0) is submitted, triggering the call sequence [take(offer_with_maker_zero, ratifierData, ...) -> isRatified -> ecrecover returns some_address -> isAuthorized[address(0)][some_address] is likely false -> Unauthorized revert], violating the invariant that offers with maker=address(0) must not be fillable, causing scoped impact: permanent freeze of any offer with zero maker address? Proof idea: unit test with offer.maker = address(0); assert Unauthorized revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that the structHash commits to the Merkle root (not individual offer hashes), so a signature over root R is valid for all trees with root R regardless of tree structure, under the precondition that two different trees (different heights, different offers) happen to have the same root R, triggering the call sequence [taker: take(offer_from_tree2, abi.encode(sig_for_tree1_root_R, R, leafIndex, proof_from_tree2), ...) -> isRatified -> isLeaf passes for tree2 -> structHash uses offerTreeTypeHash(proof.length) which differs between trees -> ecrecover fails], violating the invariant that a signature must be bound to a specific tree structure, causing scoped impact: cross-tree signature confusion causing fund freeze if height mismatch? Proof idea: construct two trees with same root but different heights; assert signature from one fails for the other.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: cancelRoot] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by calling cancelRoot(maker, root) where maker is a smart contract that has authorized the attacker, and the smart contract maker cannot re-sign offers (no private key), under the precondition that maker is a smart contract with no ability to generate new signatures, triggering the call sequence [attacker: cancelRoot(smart_contract_maker, root), smart_contract_maker: cannot re-sign -> all offers permanently frozen], violating the invariant that a smart contract maker must be able to recover from root cancellation, causing scoped impact: permanent freeze of all smart contract maker's offers with no recovery path? Proof idea: deploy smart contract maker; assert cancelRoot by authorized operator permanently freezes all offers with no recovery.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that the domain separator uses address(this) (EcrecoverRatifier address) not the Midnight address, so a signature created for EcrecoverRatifier at address A is not valid for EcrecoverRatifier at address B, under the precondition that a maker signs offers for one EcrecoverRatifier deployment and the market is configured to use a different EcrecoverRatifier deployment, triggering the call sequence [take(offer_with_ratifier_B, ratifierData_signed_for_ratifier_A, ...) -> isRatified on ratifier_B -> domain separator uses address(ratifier_B) -> ecrecover returns wrong address -> Unauthorized revert], violating the invariant that a signed offer must be fillable with the ratifier specified in the offer, causing scoped impact: permanent freeze if ratifier address in offer differs from signing target? Proof idea: deploy two EcrecoverRatifier instances; assert cross-instance signature fails.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that isRatified checks isAuthorized[offer.maker][_signer] where _signer is the ecrecover result, so if maker authorizes address X and X's private key is compromised, the attacker can sign arbitrary offers on behalf of maker, under the precondition that maker has authorized a compromised signer, triggering the call sequence [attacker: signs malicious offer with compromised key, taker: take(malicious_offer, ratifierData_signed_by_compromised_key, ...) -> isRatified passes -> malicious offer fills], violating the invariant that only the maker's intended offers must be fillable, causing scoped impact: unauthorized fills draining maker's credit or collateral, or freezing maker's funds through malicious offer terms? Proof idea: fuzz compromised signer key; assert malicious offers pass isRatified.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that hashOffer includes offer.group (bytes32), so two offers with the same parameters but different groups have different hashes and require different proofs, under the precondition that maker signs a tree with offer.group = G1 but the taker submits the offer with group = G2, triggering the call sequence [take(offer_with_group_G2, ratifierData_for_group_G1, ...) -> isRatified -> hashOffer returns different hash -> InvalidProof revert], violating the invariant that a signed offer must be fillable with the correct group, causing scoped impact: permanent freeze if group is mutated between signing and taking, blocking consumption tracking? Proof idea: fuzz offer.group; assert InvalidProof when group differs from signed value.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: cancelRoot / isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the interaction between cancelRoot and the consumption group mechanism, where canceling a root freezes all offers in a group, preventing the group's consumed counter from being reset via setConsumed, under the precondition that maker has a group of offers under one root and attacker cancels the root, triggering the call sequence [attacker: cancelRoot(maker, root), maker: setConsumed(group, 0, maker) (resets counter), taker: take(offer, ratifierData, ...) -> RootCanceled revert], violating the invariant that resetting consumed must allow re-filling offers, causing scoped impact: permanent freeze of all offers in the group even after consumed reset? Proof idea: state test; assert cancelRoot permanently blocks group offers regardless of consumed reset.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that isRatified is a view function that does not update any state, so a malicious taker can call isRatified directly (bypassing the NotMidnight check by impersonating MIDNIGHT) to probe whether a signature is valid before submitting a take, under the precondition that attacker can predict the MIDNIGHT address and call isRatified from that address, triggering the call sequence [attacker: vm.prank(MIDNIGHT), isRatified(offer, ratifierData) -> returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS], violating the invariant that isRatified must only be callable by MIDNIGHT, causing scoped impact: signature validity oracle that enables targeted griefing or front-running? Proof idea: unit test with vm.prank(MIDNIGHT); assert isRatified is callable and returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that the Merkle proof verification in isLeaf uses keccak256 of concatenated hashes (not sorted), so a second-preimage attack on the Merkle tree could allow an attacker to construct a fake leaf that passes isLeaf, under the precondition that attacker can find a 64-byte value that hashes to a valid internal node, triggering the call sequence [take(fake_offer, abi.encode(sig, root, leafIndex, fake_proof), ...) -> isRatified -> isLeaf passes with fake leaf -> ecrecover check fails if fake offer has wrong maker], violating the invariant that only offers in the signed tree must pass isLeaf, causing scoped impact: if fake offer has attacker as maker, unauthorized fills; if it causes revert, DoS? Proof idea: attempt second-preimage on Merkle tree; assert isLeaf rejects fake leaves.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that isRatified reads isAuthorized from MIDNIGHT which is an external call, and if MIDNIGHT is a contract that reverts on isAuthorized (e.g. due to a bug or upgrade), all EcrecoverRatifier-gated takes will permanently revert, under the precondition that MIDNIGHT.isAuthorized reverts for any reason, triggering the call sequence [take(offer, ratifierData, ...) -> isRatified -> IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(maker, signer) reverts -> propagates as revert in take], violating the invariant that isRatified must not be permanently broken by MIDNIGHT state, causing scoped impact: permanent freeze of all EcrecoverRatifier-gated offers if MIDNIGHT.isAuthorized is broken? Proof idea: mock MIDNIGHT with reverting isAuthorized; assert all takes permanently revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that hashOffer includes offer.tick, so a maker who signs an offer at tick T cannot have that offer filled at tick T' (even if T' is a valid tick in the same market), under the precondition that the market's tick spacing is reduced after signing (unlocking new ticks) and the taker submits the offer with a different tick, triggering the call sequence [midnight.setMarketTickSpacing(id, new_spacing), take(offer_with_different_tick, ratifierData_for_original_tick, ...) -> isRatified -> hashOffer returns different hash -> InvalidProof revert], violating the invariant that a signed offer must be fillable at the signed tick, causing scoped impact: permanent freeze if tick is mutated, blocking settlement flows? Proof idea: fuzz offer.tick; assert InvalidProof when tick differs from signed value.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: cancelRoot] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that cancelRoot emits CancelRoot event but does not check if the root was ever used or signed, so an attacker can cancel arbitrary roots including roots that maker plans to use in the future, under the precondition that attacker is authorized by maker and can enumerate all possible roots from offer parameters, triggering the call sequence [attacker: for each predicted_root in predicted_roots: cancelRoot(maker, predicted_root)], violating the invariant that a maker must be able to use any root they choose, causing scoped impact: permanent freeze of maker's ability to use any predictable root, blocking all future offer fills? Proof idea: enumerate roots from known offer params; assert all are pre-canceled and all future takes revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that isRatified does not validate that offer.ratifier == address(this), so a signature created for EcrecoverRatifier at address A could be submitted to a take where offer.ratifier = B (a different ratifier), under the precondition that Midnight checks isAuthorized[maker][offer.ratifier] and calls offer.ratifier.isRatified, triggering the call sequence [take(offer_with_ratifier_B, ratifierData_signed_for_ratifier_A, ...) -> Midnight calls B.isRatified -> B has different logic -> may pass or fail], violating the invariant that ratifierData must be bound to the specific ratifier in the offer, causing scoped impact: cross-ratifier data confusion causing fund freeze or unauthorized fills? Proof idea: submit ratifierData for ratifier A to ratifier B; assert behavior differs from expected.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that the Merkle tree height is determined by proof.length at verification time (not at signing time), so a maker who signs a root for a tree of height H can have their signature used with a proof of length H' != H (different typeHash), under the precondition that attacker constructs a tree of height H' with the same root R (hash collision across heights), triggering the call sequence [take(offer, abi.encode(sig_for_height_H, R, leafIndex, proof_height_H_prime), ...) -> isRatified -> structHash uses offerTreeTypeHash(H') -> ecrecover returns wrong address -> Unauthorized revert], violating the invariant that a signature must be bound to a specific tree height, causing scoped impact: permanent freeze if proof length is wrong, blocking offer fills? Proof idea: differential test across all heights 0-20; assert signature for height H fails for all H' != H.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the interaction between EcrecoverRatifier and Midnight's multicall, where a multicall batch includes a cancelRoot call followed by a take call, under the precondition that attacker is authorized by maker and submits a multicall with [cancelRoot(maker, root), take(offer, ratifierData, ...)], triggering the call sequence [midnight.multicall([cancelRoot_calldata, take_calldata]) -> cancelRoot succeeds -> take -> isRatified -> RootCanceled revert -> entire multicall reverts], violating the invariant that multicall must not allow atomic root cancellation and take in the same transaction, causing scoped impact: atomic griefing attempt that reverts itself but demonstrates the cancelRoot griefing vector? Proof idea: multicall test; assert cancelRoot in multicall atomically blocks take in same batch.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that isRatified is called with the offer as submitted by the taker (not as stored on-chain), so a taker can mutate any offer field that is not checked by Midnight before calling isRatified, under the precondition that Midnight passes the offer struct directly to isRatified without validation, triggering the call sequence [take(mutated_offer, ratifierData_for_original_offer, ...) -> isRatified -> hashOffer(mutated_offer) != hashOffer(original_offer) -> InvalidProof revert], violating the invariant that the offer submitted to take must exactly match the signed offer, causing scoped impact: permanent freeze if any offer field is mutated between signing and taking? Proof idea: fuzz each offer field independently; assert InvalidProof for any mutation.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: cancelRoot / isRatified] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER cause a fund freeze by exploiting the fact that isRootCanceled is a permanent boolean (no uncancellation), so once a root is canceled it can never be uncanceled, under the precondition that maker accidentally cancels a root with active offers, triggering the call sequence [maker: cancelRoot(maker, root_with_active_offers), taker: take(offer, ratifierData, ...) -> RootCanceled revert], violating the invariant that a maker must be able to recover from accidental root cancellation, causing scoped impact: permanent freeze of all offers under accidentally canceled root with no recovery path? Proof idea: unit test; assert isRootCanceled[maker][root] is permanently true after cancelRoot with no uncancellation function.
